comprehension: Who brings Heidi to the Alps at the beginning of the story?
What is Heidi's grandfather called by the villagers?
How does Heidi feel when she first sees the mountains?

informational.title: The Swiss Alps: Europe's Highest Mountains
informational.part1: The Alps are a massive mountain range that stretches across eight European countries, with the Swiss Alps being some of the most famous and beautiful. These mountains have snow-capped peaks that reach over 15,000 feet high, including the Matterhorn, one of the most recognized mountains in the world. The Alps were formed millions of years ago when massive sections of the Earth's crust collided and pushed upward, creating these towering peaks.
informational.part2: Life in the Alps has always been challenging but rewarding. Mountain villages are small and isolated, especially in winter when heavy snow blocks roads and passes. People who live in the Alps have adapted to the harsh conditions by building sturdy wooden houses, raising hardy animals like goats and sheep, and developing special skills for surviving in mountain environments. The fresh mountain air, stunning views, and close connection to nature make Alpine life special, just as Heidi discovers in her story.

61.journal: Heidi moves to a completely new place at the start of the story. Write about a time when you went somewhere new. How did it feel?
61.opinion: Do you think children should live in cities or in the countryside? What are the benefits of each?

grammar.example: Heidi traveled up the Alps to meet her grandfather.

comprehension: What kind of house does the grandfather live in?
What animals does Peter herd in the mountains?
Why do the villagers think the grandfather is unfriendly?

informational.title: Goat Herding in Mountain Regions
informational.part1: For thousands of years, people living in mountainous areas have raised goats because these animals are perfectly suited for steep, rocky terrain. Goats are excellent climbers with split hooves that grip rocky surfaces, and they can eat tough mountain plants that other animals can't digest. Goat herders, like Peter in Heidi's story, lead their herds to high mountain pastures during summer where the goats graze on fresh grass and herbs. This traditional practice is called 'Alpine pasturing.'
informational.part2: Goats provide mountain families with milk, cheese, and meat—essential foods when living far from towns and stores. Goat's milk is rich and nutritious, and it can be made into delicious cheese that lasts through long winters. The life of a goat herder requires patience, responsibility, and a deep knowledge of the mountains. Herders must protect their goats from predators, guide them to good grazing areas, and bring them safely home each evening. This important job has been passed down through generations in Alpine communities.

62.journal: Peter has an important job taking care of the goats. Write about a responsibility you have and why it matters.
62.opinion: Would you rather have a pet that lives inside your house or take care of farm animals outside? Explain your choice.

grammar.example: Heidi and Peter watched the goats on the mountain.

comprehension: What does Heidi learn from her grandfather?
How does Heidi spend her days in the Alps?
What makes Heidi happy living with her grandfather?

informational.title: Life Without Electricity: Living Simply
informational.part1: When Heidi's story takes place in the late 1800s, many people in remote mountain areas lived without electricity, running water, or modern conveniences. Families relied on natural resources: they burned wood for heat and cooking, used candles or oil lamps for light, and carried water from mountain springs or wells. Everything had to be done by hand—baking bread, making clothes, preserving food for winter, and caring for animals. This required hard work and careful planning.
informational.part2: Despite the challenges, simple mountain life had many benefits. Families were self-sufficient, meaning they produced most of what they needed themselves. They ate fresh, natural foods and breathed clean air. Without televisions, phones, or computers, people spent time together, telling stories, playing music, and enjoying nature. Many people today appreciate this simpler way of life and seek moments to disconnect from technology and reconnect with nature, just as Heidi did on her mountain.

63.journal: Imagine living without electricity for a whole day. What would you do differently? What would you miss most?
63.opinion: Is it better to have many toys and games, or just a few things you really love? Explain your thinking.

grammar.example: Heidi climbs the mountain every morning.

comprehension: Who is Peter and what does he do?
How does Heidi make friends with Peter?
What do Heidi and Peter do together in the mountains?

informational.title: Friendship and Social Skills
informational.part1: Making friends is one of the most important skills children develop. Friends provide companionship, support, and opportunities to learn social skills like sharing, taking turns, resolving conflicts, and understanding others' feelings. Research shows that having friends improves children's happiness, reduces stress, and helps them learn to cooperate and communicate. Friendships teach us empathy—the ability to understand how someone else feels.
informational.part2: The friendship between Heidi and Peter shows how friends can be very different yet still care about each other. Heidi is curious, energetic, and loves learning, while Peter is more practical, comfortable with routine, and connected to his mountain traditions. Despite their differences, they enjoy spending time together, help each other, and learn from one another. Good friendships aren't about being exactly alike—they're about respecting differences, finding common interests, and treating each other with kindness.

64.journal: What makes someone a good friend? Write about the qualities you value most in your friendships.
64.opinion: Is it better to have one best friend or several friends? Why?

grammar.example: Peter's goats grazed on the mountainside.

comprehension: What does Dete want Heidi to do?
Why does Heidi have to leave the mountains?
How does Heidi feel about leaving her grandfather?

informational.title: Moving and Change in Children's Lives
informational.part1: Moving to a new place is one of the biggest changes children can experience. Whether moving to a new house, city, or country, this transition involves leaving behind familiar places, routines, and friends while adapting to new surroundings. Children may feel excited about new opportunities but also sad, anxious, or angry about leaving what they know. These mixed emotions are completely normal and healthy.
informational.part2: How children cope with moving depends partly on how adults help them through the transition. Talking about feelings, maintaining some familiar routines, keeping in touch with old friends, and focusing on positive aspects of the new situation all help children adjust. Many children discover they are more adaptable than they thought—they make new friends, find new favorite places, and eventually feel at home. In Heidi's story, being forced to move without understanding why is especially difficult, teaching us the importance of explaining changes to children and considering their feelings.

66.journal: Heidi has to leave her beloved mountains. Write about something important you had to leave behind and how you felt.
66.opinion: Should adults always explain to children why they have to do something, or should children sometimes just obey? Explain your reasoning.

grammar.example: "You must come with me," said Dete to Heidi.

comprehension: Where does Dete take Heidi?
Who is Clara and why does she need a companion?
How is Frankfurt different from the mountains?

informational.title: City Life vs. Country Life
informational.part1: Cities and rural areas offer very different living experiences. Cities like Frankfurt, where Heidi goes in the story, are densely populated with tall buildings, busy streets, shops, museums, theaters, and many people. Cities provide access to excellent schools, hospitals, jobs, and cultural opportunities. However, cities can also be noisy, crowded, and polluted, with less access to nature and open space. People often live in apartments rather than houses with yards.
informational.part2: Rural or country areas, like Heidi's mountain home, have fewer people, more open space, and closer connections to nature. Life moves at a slower pace, and communities tend to be smaller and tighter-knit where everyone knows their neighbors. However, rural areas may have fewer schools, hospitals, and job opportunities, requiring travel to cities for certain services. Neither lifestyle is better—they're simply different. The best choice depends on what matters most to each person: access to cultural activities and opportunities, or space, nature, and a quieter pace of life.

67.journal: Would you rather live in a big city or in the countryside? Describe your ideal home and surroundings.
67.opinion: Is it fair to make a child move to a place they don't want to go? When might it be necessary?

grammar.example: Frankfurt was much bigger than Heidi's mountain village.

comprehension: What are the rules in Clara's house?
Who is Fräulein Rottenmeier and what is her job?
Why does Heidi find city life difficult?

informational.title: Social Rules and Etiquette
informational.part1: Every culture and household has rules about polite behavior, called etiquette. These social rules help people know how to act in different situations and show respect for others. In wealthy European homes during the 1800s, like Clara's house in Heidi, etiquette was very formal and strict. Children were expected to dress properly, speak politely, sit up straight, use correct table manners, and never interrupt adults. Breaking these rules was considered rude and unacceptable.
informational.part2: While some etiquette rules seem overly strict today, the basic idea behind them—treating others with respect and consideration—remains important. Modern etiquette is generally more relaxed, but we still have social rules: saying 'please' and 'thank you,' listening when others speak, respecting personal space, and being considerate of others' feelings. The challenge Heidi faces is learning unfamiliar rules while living in an environment very different from her mountain home. Her story teaches us to be patient with people who are new to our customs and to remember that different places have different rules.

68.journal: Write about a time when you had to learn new rules or ways of doing things. How did it feel?
68.opinion: Are strict rules helpful or harmful for children? Should there be lots of rules or just a few important ones?

grammar.example: Heidi must learn to behave properly at the dinner table.

comprehension: How does Clara feel about Heidi's arrival?
What makes Clara and Heidi different from each other?
Why is Clara confined to a wheelchair?

informational.title: Understanding Physical Disabilities
informational.part1: A physical disability is a condition that affects a person's movement, strength, or control of their body. Some people are born with disabilities, while others develop them due to illness or injury, as Clara did in the Heidi story. Physical disabilities can affect different parts of the body: legs, arms, muscles, or the spine. People with mobility disabilities might use wheelchairs, crutches, walkers, or other assistive devices to help them move around. In Clara's time, understanding of disabilities and available treatments were limited compared to today.
informational.part2: It's important to remember that having a physical disability doesn't change who a person is inside. People with disabilities have the same feelings, thoughts, dreams, and abilities as everyone else—they simply navigate the world differently. Today, laws protect people with disabilities and ensure they have access to education, public spaces, and opportunities. Ramps, elevators, accessible bathrooms, and other accommodations help people with disabilities participate fully in society. The key lesson from Heidi and Clara's friendship is to look beyond physical differences and appreciate each person for who they are.

69.journal: Clara can't walk but has many other abilities and interests. Write about something you're good at that makes you special.
69.opinion: Should buildings and schools be designed to be accessible for everyone, including people who use wheelchairs? Why or why not?

grammar.example: Clara could not walk, but she could read and tell wonderful stories.

comprehension: What does Heidi miss most about the mountains?
How does Heidi try to see outside from the house?
Why can't Heidi adjust to her new life?

informational.title: Homesickness and Missing What We Love
informational.part1: Homesickness is a strong feeling of longing for home and familiar places, people, and routines. It's a real emotion that can cause sadness, anxiety, loss of appetite, and trouble sleeping. Homesickness often happens when people move to new places, go away to camp or school, or travel far from home. Children are especially vulnerable to homesickness because their attachment to home, family, and familiar environments is so strong. In Heidi's story, her homesickness for the mountains becomes so severe it affects her health.
informational.part2: Dealing with homesickness requires patience and healthy coping strategies. Talking about feelings, keeping connections with home (through letters, calls, or photos), establishing new routines, and gradually making connections in the new place all help. Sometimes, as in Heidi's case, the homesickness signals that the new environment truly isn't the right fit, and returning home is the best solution. Other times, with support and time, the new place begins to feel comfortable. The key is recognizing that homesickness is a normal response to change, not a weakness or failure.

71.journal: Have you ever felt homesick or missed something very much? Describe what you missed and how you felt.
71.opinion: Should someone who is very homesick be allowed to return home, or should they have to stay and adjust? What factors matter?

grammar.example: Heidi was longing for the mountains every day.

comprehension: What strange things start happening at night?
Who or what is causing the mysterious events?
Why is everyone in the house frightened?

informational.title: Sleepwalking: When People Walk in Their Sleep
informational.part1: Sleepwalking, also called somnambulism, is a sleep disorder where people walk or perform activities while still asleep. Sleepwalkers typically have their eyes open and can navigate around obstacles, but they're not actually awake or aware of what they're doing. Sleepwalking is most common in children, though adults can sleepwalk too. Episodes usually happen during deep sleep in the first few hours after falling asleep. In the morning, sleepwalkers usually don't remember what they did.
informational.part2: Sleepwalking can be triggered by stress, anxiety, lack of sleep, fever, or emotional upset—all things Heidi experiences in the story. While sleepwalking itself isn't usually dangerous, there are risks: sleepwalkers might trip, fall down stairs, or try to leave the house. The best response is to gently guide the person back to bed without waking them abruptly. Heidi's sleepwalking reveals how deeply unhappy and homesick she is, signaling to the adults that something is seriously wrong. Today, doctors can help people who sleepwalk frequently through better sleep habits and, when necessary, medical treatment.

72.journal: Heidi's sleepwalking shows she's very unhappy even though she doesn't say it directly. How can we tell when someone is upset even if they don't say so?
72.opinion: Should we always listen to our feelings and emotions, or should we sometimes ignore them and push through difficult situations?

grammar.example: Strange, mysterious footsteps echoed through the dark hallway.

comprehension: What does the doctor discover about Heidi?
What does the doctor say Heidi needs?
Why does Heidi need to return to the mountains?

informational.title: The Connection Between Mind and Body
informational.part1: Our mental and emotional health directly affects our physical health—a connection doctors call the mind-body connection. When people experience prolonged sadness, stress, or anxiety, it can cause real physical symptoms: headaches, stomachaches, loss of appetite, trouble sleeping, fatigue, and weakened immunity making them sick more often. In Heidi's case, her extreme homesickness and unhappiness in Frankfurt manifest as physical illness that modern doctors would recognize as depression.
informational.part2: Understanding this connection is important for maintaining overall health. Just as we care for our bodies through good nutrition, exercise, and sleep, we must also care for our mental and emotional well-being. Activities like spending time in nature, being with loved ones, doing things we enjoy, expressing our feelings, and getting help when we're struggling all promote both mental and physical health. The doctor in Heidi's story recognizes that no medicine will cure Heidi—she needs to return to the environment where she feels happy, safe, and connected. This highlights an important truth: sometimes the best medicine isn't a pill but a change in circumstances.

73.journal: What activities or places make you feel happy and healthy? How do you know when you need to do something that's good for your emotional well-being?
73.opinion: Is mental and emotional health just as important as physical health, or is one more important than the other?

grammar.example: Because Heidi was so homesick, she became sick and needed to go home.

comprehension: How does Clara feel about Heidi leaving?
What promise does Heidi make to Clara?
What changes when Heidi prepares to return home?

informational.title: Long-Distance Friendships
informational.part1: When friends must separate and live far apart, it can be painful and challenging to maintain the friendship. Before modern technology, long-distance friendships relied entirely on letter writing. People would write letters sharing news, feelings, and experiences, then wait weeks or even months for a reply. In Heidi's time, letters were the only way to stay connected when separated by distance. Despite the delays, letter writing could create deep, meaningful connections as people carefully expressed their thoughts and feelings in writing.
informational.part2: Today, technology makes long-distance friendship much easier through phone calls, video chats, texting, and social media. Despite these advantages, maintaining long-distance friendships still requires effort and commitment from both people. The key to successful long-distance friendship is regular communication, making time to connect despite busy schedules, sharing important moments, and planning visits when possible. Heidi and Clara's friendship proves that true friends can stay connected even when separated by mountains and miles. Physical distance doesn't have to end a friendship if both people value the relationship and make efforts to maintain it.

74.journal: Have you ever had to say goodbye to a friend who moved away? How did you stay connected, or what do you wish you had done?
74.opinion: Can long-distance friendships be as strong as friendships with people you see every day? Why or why not?

grammar.example: Heidi promised, 'I will write to you every week.'

comprehension: How does Heidi feel when she returns to the mountains?
How has her grandfather changed since she left?
What has Peter been doing while Heidi was away?

informational.title: Returning Home: Coming Full Circle
informational.part1: Returning home after being away—whether for a few days or a long time—can bring powerful emotions. People often feel relief, happiness, and comfort returning to familiar places and routines. Home provides a sense of security, belonging, and connection to our identity. When Heidi returns to her mountains, she experiences immediate joy and healing, showing how important our environment and sense of place are to our well-being. Sometimes we don't fully appreciate home until we've been away from it.
informational.part2: However, returning home can also be complicated. Sometimes we've changed during our time away, or home itself has changed. We might see our home with new eyes, noticing both its positive aspects and its limitations. Heidi returns with expanded experiences and knowledge from Frankfurt, but her love for the mountains is unchanged. The best homecomings combine the comfort of familiar people and places with the growth and learning gained from new experiences. Home isn't just a location—it's wherever we feel most like ourselves and most deeply connected to what matters.

76.journal: Describe what 'home' means to you. Is it a place, people, feelings, or something else?
76.opinion: Is it better never to leave home, or to go away and come back with new experiences and appreciation?

grammar.example: How wonderful it was to be back in the mountains!

comprehension: What news does Heidi share about Clara?
How does the grandfather feel about Clara's situation?
What plan begins to form for helping Clara?

informational.title: The Healing Power of Nature
informational.part1: Spending time in nature has been proven to improve both physical and mental health. Research shows that being outdoors reduces stress, lowers blood pressure, improves mood, increases energy, and strengthens the immune system. Natural environments provide fresh air, sunlight (which helps our bodies produce vitamin D), opportunities for physical activity, and peaceful settings that calm our minds. The Japanese practice of 'forest bathing'—simply spending mindful time among trees—has become recognized worldwide for its health benefits.
informational.part2: Mountain environments, like where Heidi lives, offer particularly beneficial conditions: clean air, stunning views, peaceful quiet, and opportunities for hiking and physical activity. The combination of altitude, fresh air, and natural beauty can have remarkable effects on health and well-being. In Heidi's story, the healing power of the mountain environment is central to the plot—it cures Heidi's homesickness and, eventually, plays a role in Clara's recovery. While not everyone can live in mountains, making time to experience nature regularly, whether in parks, forests, beaches, or gardens, contributes to overall health and happiness.

77.journal: Write about a time when being outside in nature made you feel better. What was it about that place that helped you?
77.opinion: Should people spend more time outdoors and less time inside with screens and technology? Why or why not?

grammar.example: The fresh mountain air filled Heidi's lungs as she climbed the sunny meadow.

comprehension: What preparations are made for Clara's visit?
How does Peter feel about Clara coming?
Why is everyone excited (or worried) about Clara's arrival?

informational.title: Welcoming Guests and Hospitality
informational.part1: Hospitality—making guests feel welcome and comfortable—is valued in cultures worldwide. Good hospitality involves preparing for guests' arrival, greeting them warmly, providing for their needs, and making them feel at home in your space. In different cultures, hospitality takes various forms: offering food and drink, providing comfortable accommodations, showing guests around, and giving them attention and respect. In mountain communities like Heidi's, where life is simple, hospitality might mean sharing simple meals and a clean place to sleep, but offering it with genuine warmth.
informational.part2: Preparing for a guest with special needs, like Clara, requires extra thought and planning. Making sure the space is accessible, considering what assistance might be needed, and adapting activities so everyone can participate all show thoughtful hospitality. The goal is to help guests feel comfortable and valued despite any challenges. Good hosts also help other people interact appropriately with guests who may be different from them. In Heidi's story, preparing for Clara's visit teaches the mountain community about inclusion and accommodation while offering Clara new experiences she could never have in Frankfurt.

78.journal: What does it mean to make someone feel welcome? Describe how you can be a good host to a friend or visitor.
78.opinion: When someone visits your home, should you change your normal routines to accommodate them, or should guests adapt to your way of doing things?

grammar.example: They prepared a room for Clara on the lower floor of the house.

comprehension: How does Clara react to seeing the mountains?
What challenges does Clara face in the mountain environment?
How do Heidi and her grandfather help Clara adjust?

informational.title: Adapting to New Environments
informational.part1: Adapting to a dramatically different environment—especially for someone with physical challenges—requires creativity, patience, and flexibility. Clara, accustomed to flat city streets and accessible buildings, must navigate the steep, uneven mountain terrain. However, new environments also offer opportunities: fresh experiences, different perspectives, and chances to discover hidden strengths. Adaptation often involves finding new ways to do familiar activities and discovering entirely new activities that weren't possible before.
informational.part2: Successful adaptation depends on several factors: physical accessibility, social support, attitude, and willingness to try. When people help by providing assistance, making modifications, and encouraging attempts, adaptation becomes easier. But the individual's own attitude matters too—approaching challenges with curiosity and determination rather than fear and resistance. Clara's mountain visit becomes transformative not just because of the physical environment, but because she's willing to try new things with support from Heidi and her grandfather. This combination of helpful support and personal courage enables people to thrive in new situations.

79.journal: Write about a time when you had to adapt to something new and different. What helped you adjust?
79.opinion: Is it better to stay in comfortable, familiar situations or to challenge yourself with new experiences?

grammar.example: First, they carried Clara up the mountain. Then, they showed her the meadow. Finally, she saw the goats.

comprehension: What happens to Clara's wheelchair?
Why does Peter destroy the wheelchair?
How does this event change Clara's situation?

informational.title: Jealousy and Managing Difficult Emotions
informational.part1: Jealousy is a complex emotion that arises when we feel threatened by someone or something taking away attention, affection, or resources we want. Peter's jealousy of Clara stems from feeling that Clara is taking Heidi's time and attention away from him. Jealousy can lead to destructive behavior—as Peter demonstrates by destroying Clara's wheelchair—even though the person knows it's wrong. Recognizing jealousy in ourselves is the first step to managing it appropriately.
informational.part2: Healthy ways to handle jealousy include: recognizing and naming the feeling, talking about it with trusted people, remembering that relationships aren't competitions (Heidi can care about both Peter and Clara), and addressing the underlying need (Peter needs reassurance that he's still important to Heidi). Destructive responses to jealousy—like sabotage, gossiping, or hurting others—ultimately make situations worse and damage relationships. Peter's action has serious consequences, but it also forces honest conversation about feelings. Learning to manage jealousy and other difficult emotions is a crucial life skill.

81.journal: Have you ever felt jealous of someone? What caused the feeling, and how did you handle it?
81.opinion: Should Peter be punished for destroying Clara's wheelchair, or should people understand he acted out of jealousy? Can both be true?

grammar.example: Peter pushed the wheelchair down the mountain, and it crashed into pieces.

comprehension: How must Clara move around without her wheelchair?
What does Heidi encourage Clara to try?
How does Clara begin to get stronger?

informational.title: Physical Therapy and Strengthening Muscles
informational.part1: Physical therapy is treatment that helps people improve movement, strength, and physical function, especially after injury or illness. Physical therapists design exercises and activities to strengthen weak muscles, improve balance, increase flexibility, and help people regain abilities they may have lost. When muscles aren't used, they become weak—a condition called atrophy. This is what happened to Clara during her years in the wheelchair. Regular, gentle exercise can rebuild muscle strength gradually.
informational.part2: The process requires patience, consistency, and gradual progression. You can't rebuild strength overnight—it takes weeks or months of regular practice. Starting with small goals (like standing with support, taking a few steps, or walking short distances) and slowly increasing difficulty prevents injury and builds confidence. Encouragement and support from others makes the difficult work of physical rehabilitation easier. In Heidi's time, formal physical therapy didn't exist as we know it today, but Heidi's encouragement and the motivation provided by the beautiful mountain environment help Clara persist in strengthening her legs through natural movement and walking.

82.journal: Write about something difficult you practiced until you got better at it. How did it feel when you finally succeeded?
82.opinion: Sometimes people need to be pushed to try difficult things, and sometimes they should be allowed to decide for themselves. When should people encourage others to try, and when should they let people make their own choices?

grammar.example: Clara was slowly learning to walk with Heidi's help.

comprehension: What milestone does Clara achieve?
How do Heidi and the grandfather help Clara succeed?
What role does determination play in Clara's progress?

informational.title: The Power of Determination and Perseverance
informational.part1: Determination—the quality of being firmly committed to achieving a goal despite difficulties—is one of the most important factors in success. Psychologists call this 'grit': the combination of passion and perseverance toward long-term goals. Research shows that grit often matters more than talent or intelligence in achieving difficult goals. People with determination don't give up when things get hard; they view challenges as opportunities to grow and learn rather than as reasons to quit.
informational.part2: Clara's journey from wheelchair-bound to walking demonstrates determination in action. Every attempt to stand or walk is difficult and sometimes painful, but she keeps trying. Several factors support her determination: Heidi's encouragement, her grandfather's patient support, the inspiring mountain environment, and her own desire to experience the mountains fully. This combination of internal motivation and external support creates the conditions for success. Clara's story teaches that while we can't always control our circumstances, we can control our effort and attitude—and sometimes that makes all the difference.

83.journal: Write about a goal you worked hard to achieve. What kept you going when it was difficult?
83.opinion: Which matters more for success: natural talent and ability, or hard work and determination? Why?

grammar.example: Taking her first steps was the happiest moment of Clara's life.

comprehension: Who arrives from Frankfurt to visit?
What surprise awaits them in the mountains?
How do people react to Clara's progress?

informational.title: Medical Surprises and Unexpected Healing
informational.part1: Throughout medical history, there have been documented cases of unexpected recoveries that doctors didn't predict. Sometimes people recover from conditions thought to be permanent or hopeless. While modern medicine can explain many of these 'miraculous' recoveries—through the body's healing abilities, the power of proper conditions, or misdiagnosis—they still inspire wonder. In Clara's case, her doctors believed she would never walk again, but the combination of mountain air, proper nutrition, gentle exercise, strong motivation, and emotional support create conditions for healing.
informational.part2: This doesn't mean everyone with disabilities will recover through positive thinking or environmental change—disabilities are real, and most are permanent. However, Clara's story highlights important truths: medical knowledge continues to evolve, second opinions and alternative approaches can be valuable, environment and emotional state affect physical health, and human resilience can be remarkable. Today's medicine recognizes the importance of treating the whole person—physical, emotional, and environmental factors—rather than just the specific medical condition. Clara's recovery teaches us to maintain hope while being realistic, and to explore all possibilities for health and healing.

84.journal: Have you ever experienced something turning out much better than you expected? Describe what happened and how it felt.
84.opinion: Should we always believe what doctors say, or is it sometimes worth seeking second opinions and trying different approaches?

grammar.example: The doctor said, 'I cannot believe Clara is walking!' He said he could not believe Clara was walking.

comprehension: What decision does Clara's father make?
How has Clara changed during her mountain stay?
What does the future hold for the characters?

informational.title: Personal Growth and Transformation
informational.part1: Personal growth refers to the process of developing new skills, attitudes, and understanding about ourselves and the world. Major growth often happens during challenging experiences that push us outside our comfort zones. Clara grows tremendously during her mountain stay: physically (learning to walk), emotionally (becoming braver and more independent), and socially (forming deep friendships and learning to appreciate simpler pleasures). These changes happen because she's in a new environment that challenges her assumptions about what's possible.
informational.part2: Growth requires several elements: challenge (being pushed beyond current abilities), support (people who encourage and believe in us), safe space to try and fail, and time to practice and improve. The mountain environment provides all these elements for Clara. Her transformation teaches important lessons: we're capable of more than we think, changing our environment can change our possibilities, support from caring people makes growth possible, and perseverance through difficulty leads to reward. Personal growth is often uncomfortable in the moment but deeply satisfying when we look back and see how far we've come.

86.journal: Think about how you've grown or changed in the past year. What experiences helped you grow? What did you learn about yourself?
86.opinion: Is it better to stay comfortable and safe, or to take risks that might help you grow even though they're scary?

grammar.example: Clara had been afraid, but she learned to be brave in the mountains.

comprehension: How does the story end for Heidi and Clara?
What happens to Peter?
What lessons have the characters learned?

informational.title: Friendship Changes Lives
informational.part1: The story of Heidi and Clara demonstrates how friendship can profoundly impact our lives. True friends help us become better versions of ourselves. Heidi's friendship gives Clara courage to try walking, experiences she never had in Frankfurt, and understanding that life holds more possibilities than she imagined. Clara's friendship teaches Heidi about different ways of living, the value of education and reading, and compassion for people facing challenges. Together, they help each other grow in ways neither could have grown alone.
informational.part2: The best friendships are transformative—they change us for the better. Good friends challenge us to try new things, support us through difficulties, celebrate our successes, and accept us as we are while encouraging us to grow. The Heidi story also shows that circumstances often bring people together unexpectedly, and friendships can form between people who seem very different. We don't choose friends based on similarity alone—we connect with people who bring out the best in us, make us laugh, understand us, and share experiences that create bonds stronger than any difference.

87.journal: Write about a friend who has helped you grow or become better in some way. How did they help you?
87.opinion: Can children and adults learn important lessons from fairy tales and stories, or are they just entertainment?

grammar.example: The story teaches us that friendship, nature, and determination can heal and transform lives.

comprehension: What makes this story memorable?
Which character changed the most and why?
What is the most important message of the story?

informational.title: Classic Literature for Children
informational.part1: 'Heidi' by Johanna Spyri, first published in 1881, is one of the world's most beloved children's books. It has been translated into more than 50 languages and adapted into numerous films, TV series, and plays. The story's lasting appeal comes from its universal themes: the importance of home and belonging, the healing power of nature, friendship across differences, overcoming physical challenges, and the resilience of children. These themes resonate with readers across cultures and time periods.
informational.part2: Classic children's literature like 'Heidi' serves multiple purposes: it entertains with engaging stories, teaches valuable lessons about life and human nature, introduces children to different places and cultures, builds reading skills and vocabulary, and provides shared cultural references that connect generations. Many people who read 'Heidi' as children remember it fondly as adults and share it with their own children. These stories become part of our cultural heritage, teaching each new generation timeless truths about courage, kindness, perseverance, and the human spirit. Reading classic literature connects us to millions of readers across history who found the same stories meaningful.

88.journal: What is your favorite book or story? Why does it mean something special to you?
88.opinion: Is it important to read old classic books, or should we focus on new modern stories? What are the benefits of each?

grammar.example: Heidi was written by Johanna Spyri and published in Switzerland in 1881.

comprehension: How did reading this story make you feel?
What part of the story did you find most interesting or surprising?
Would you want to visit the Swiss Alps after reading about Heidi's adventures?

informational.title: Switzerland: The Country Behind the Story
informational.part1: Switzerland is a small European country famous for its stunning Alpine mountains, precision watches, delicious chocolate, and political neutrality. The Swiss Alps, where Heidi lives, cover about 60% of the country. Switzerland has four official languages (German, French, Italian, and Romansh) reflecting its diverse cultural regions. The country is known for its high quality of life, excellent education system, and beautiful natural environment. Many tourists visit Switzerland each year to ski, hike, and experience the mountain villages that inspired stories like Heidi.
informational.part2: Swiss mountain culture, which Heidi represents, values self-sufficiency, hard work, community cooperation, and connection to nature. Traditional Alpine life involved making cheese, farming on steep mountainsides, raising livestock, and enduring harsh winters. Today, many Swiss mountain villages balance maintaining these traditions with modern tourism and conveniences. The values of independence, quality craftsmanship, and harmony with nature that characterized traditional Swiss mountain life remain important to Swiss identity today. Johanna Spyri's 'Heidi' brought worldwide attention to Swiss Alpine culture and remains one of Switzerland's best-known cultural exports.

89.journal: If you could visit any place mentioned in the Heidi story, where would you go and why?
89.opinion: Does reading stories about other countries and cultures help us understand and appreciate different ways of life? How?

grammar.example: Heidi lived in the Swiss Alps in Switzerland, and Clara came from Frankfurt in Germany.
